title: TCP/IP Model (Internet Protocol Suite)
description: The practical 4-layer model that powers the modern internet, developed by DARPA in the 1970s.
fullExplanation: The TCP/IP model (also called the Internet Protocol Suite or DoD model) is the actual protocol suite used on the internet. Unlike OSI which is a theoretical reference model, TCP/IP is the real implementation. It has 4 layers: Application (combines OSI layers 5-7), Transport (OSI layer 4), Internet (OSI layer 3), and Network Access/Link (OSI layers 1-2). Every device on the internet uses TCP/IP. The name comes from its two most important protocols: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) and IP (Internet Protocol).

layers.name: Application Layer
layers.description: Provides network services directly to applications. Combines OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers.

layers.name: Transport Layer
layers.description: Provides end-to-end communication. TCP is reliable and connection-oriented. UDP is fast and connectionless.

layers.name: Internet Layer
layers.description: Routes packets across networks using IP addresses. Handles fragmentation, reassembly, and routing.

layers.name: Network Access Layer (Link Layer)
layers.description: Handles physical transmission over network hardware. Combines OSI's Physical and Data Link layers.

keyFacts: Developed by DARPA in 1970s for ARPANET (precursor to internet)
4 layers vs OSI's 7 layers - more practical and simpler
TCP/IP is the actual internet protocol suite used worldwide
OSI is a reference model for teaching, TCP/IP is the real implementation
Named after TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) and IP (Internet Protocol)
IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses
Application layer combines OSI layers 5, 6, and 7
Network Access layer combines OSI layers 1 and 2
